summarization.a: The heatmap displays the instantaneous velocity fields in the reactor for a flow with Reynolds number (Re) of 40. There is only laminar flow.
summarization.b: The heatmap displays the instantaneous velocity fields in the reactor for a flow with Reynolds number (Re) of 80. There is only laminar flow.
summarization.c: The heatmap displays the instantaneous velocity fields in the reactor for a flow with Reynolds number (Re) of 200. There are some features visible  in the middle related to turbulant flows.
summarization.d: The heatmap displays the instantaneous velocity fields in the reactor for a flow with Reynolds number (Re) of 800. There are features visible  in the middle related to turbulant flows.
summarization.e: The heatmap displays the instantaneous velocity fields in the reactor for a flow with Reynolds number (Re) of 2400. There are features visible related to turbulant flows, already from the inlet.

e.question: Can it be bad for the reactor if there is turbulance already in the inlet pipe?
e.answer: e	Yes this is bad for the reactor as it can lead to backflow of chemicals into the lines.

a.question: Why are there low spots between the inlet places?
a.answer: a	It could be that the flow is bounced back from the grid support, but as it can not go back into the stream it will divert into the middle.

d.question: Does the turbulance necessarily mean that there will be bad uniformity?
d.answer: d	No this does not necessarily have to be the case. As long as the exposure time is long enough to also fully cover the less dense areas.

b.question: Is there turbulance observed with a Reynolds number of 80?
